questions.prompt: A team built an agent for marketing emails — 6 tools, average 5 steps, 8s latency, $0.10/run. Audit shows 90% of runs follow the same path. What's the right move?

choices.text: Rewrite as a workflow: classify intent, fetch data, draft email, send. Deterministic steps, 2 LLM calls, ~2s latency, ~$0.025/run. Reserve agents for tasks that genuinely require autonomy — when steps depend on intermediate results in ways no fixed sequence can cover.

questions.explanation: Agents are powerful for open-ended problems but expensive and slower for predictable ones. If 90% of runs take the same path, that's a workflow, not an agent. Promote to agent only when you genuinely need autonomy — and consider hybrid patterns (workflow with agent sub-steps) for the 10% of complex cases.

questions.prompt: When is an agent the right pattern over a workflow?

choices.text: Whenever there are multiple LLM calls.

When the steps depend on intermediate results in ways a fixed sequence can't cover; when the problem is genuinely open-ended (research, exploration, multi-tool synthesis); when a workflow would have too many branches to maintain. For narrow, predictable tasks, a workflow is faster, cheaper, and more reliable.
Whenever there are tools to call.
Whenever the user asks for an agent.

questions.explanation: Agents earn their cost when the value is the system figuring out what to do — branching dynamically based on results. Workflows win whenever the steps are predictable. Default to workflow; reach for an agent only when the workflow runs out of expressiveness.
